answer:
The metaphorical pair of *rah* (path, road, or journey) and *manzil* (destination or stage) does not appear verbatim using those Urdu terms (or direct English calques like "way" and "destination") across the translations in this corpus. Searches for the original terms, their Romanizations, and conceptual English equivalents yielded no literal matches in Ghalib or Zauq, and only one explicit conceptual pairing in Iqbal.[^1] The corpus translations prioritize poetic naturalness over literal terms, so the *idea* of endless journeying toward (or questioning) an endpoint recurs thematically in Sufi-influenced Urdu ghazals/nazms, but not as a fixed refrain.

### Iqbal (Bang-E-Dara Part 2, "Chaand aur Taare")
The stars lament their ceaseless motion from dusk to dawn and explicitly question if the "great journey" will ever end or yield a "destination." The moon responds that life's essence is ceaseless motion on "this path," where pausing invites death—echoing *rah* as eternal striving, *manzil* as elusive beauty.[^2]

> Will this great journey ever cease?  
> Will a **destination** meet our gaze?[^3]  
> ...  
> To pause upon **this path** is but a flaw,  
> For hidden in Stillness is death's own law.[^4]

### Ghalib (Diwan-e-Ghalib, Ghazal 100)
"Don't mock my **path** toward the holy site" (*Kaaba*, a sacred *manzil*) contrasts the pilgrim's road with lost tavern fellowship, implying virtue's endpoint amid fate's deviations. *Rah* is literal/spiritual journey; *manzil* inferred as sanctuary.[^5]

> Don't mock **my path** toward the holy site, you see,  
> The tavern's fellowship is lost to me.[^6]

### Ghalib (Diwan-e-Ghalib, Ghazal 195)
The lover journeys to the beloved's street (*manzil* as endpoint), fainting upon arrival; later, hope draws him along "the **road**" toward grace's shrine. *Rah* is arduous travel; *manzil* as anticipated (yet overwhelming) arrival.[^7]

> A single hope, O Ghalib, leads me onward past despair;  
> **The road** I walk, drawn by the hook of Grace's holy name...[^8]

No clear instance of the pair appears in *Diwan-e-Zauq* despite scanning wiki cards/full sheets and targeted searches—Zauq favors "way" in isolation (e.g., "faithless way," "human way") without paired endpoint imagery.[^9] The concept is consistent where present: *rah* evokes striving/journey (physical, spiritual, or erotic); *manzil* (implied) as goal/arrival, with no semantic shift across authors.[^1]

[^1]: *Allama Iqbal, Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 9, [full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/09-chaand-aur-taare/translation/).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-a7f06e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/09-chaand-aur-taare/translation/#p-a7f06e).
[^3]: Ibid.
[^4]: Ibid., [p-fb94a9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/09-chaand-aur-taare/translation/#p-fb94a9).
[^5]: *Mirza Ghalib, Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 100, [full chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/100-kaaba-mein-jaa-raha-toh-na-do-tana-kya-kahein/translation/).
[^6]: Ibid., [p-fa6504](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/100-kaaba-mein-jaa-raha-toh-na-do-tana-kya-kahein/translation/#p-fa6504).
[^7]: *Mirza Ghalib, Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 195, [full chapter](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/195-waan-pahunch-kar-jo-ghash-aata-paye-ham-hai-hum-ko/translation-2/).
[^8]: Ibid., [p-30dc4d](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/195-waan-pahunch-kar-jo-ghash-aata-paye-ham-hai-hum-ko/translation-2/#p-30dc4d).
[^9]: *Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, Diwan-E-Zauq*, [wiki card](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/wiki/_work.card.md).
